items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.

items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.
It's SELECT. “UPDATE” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does a primary key guarantee?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Uniqueness of each row. “Sorted data” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many primary keys can a single table have?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 key. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
